questions.specification: Two planes, P and Q, depart from the same airport on different bearings. Plane P flies on a bearing of $045^\circ$ for 150 km, and plane Q flies on a bearing of $135^\circ$ for 200 km.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Geometry and Trigonometry > SL 3.3—Angles of elevation and depression > Question Type 2: Exploring complex formulations using bearings and angles
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 2: Exploring complex formulations using bearings and angles
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Both parts use bearings to form angles between paths and then apply trigonometry (e.g., cosine rule) to find distance/angle between directions, which matches complex bearings/angles applications.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Geometry and Trigonometry > SL 3.3—Angles of elevation and depression > Question Type 1: Finding angles using bearings
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 1: Finding angles using bearings
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (b) specifically asks for the smallest angle between two bearings (direction lines), i.e., finding an angle using bearings.

parts.content: Calculate the distance between the two planes after completing their flights.
parts.markscheme:
- **Identify** the angle between the paths: $135^\circ - 045^\circ = 90^\circ$ <marks>M1</marks>
- **Substitute** values into the Pythagorean theorem: $d^2 = 150^2 + 200^2$ <marks>A1</marks>
- **Calculate** the final distance: $d = \sqrt{62500} = 250$ km <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

parts.content: Another plane F is travelling on a bearing of $280^\circ$. Determine the smallest angle between the path of plane F and the path of plane Q.
parts.markscheme:
- **Calculate** the difference between the bearings of F and Q: $280^\circ - 135^\circ = 145^\circ$ <marks>M1</marks>
- **Identify** the two possible angles (clockwise and counter-clockwise): $145^\circ$ and $360^\circ - 145^\circ = 215^\circ$ <marks>A1</marks>
- **State** the smallest angle: $145^\circ$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

questions.specification: Two ships, A and B, leave a port, travelling in different directions. Ship A travels $60$ km on a bearing of $030^\circ$, while ship B travels $80$ km on a bearing of $120^\circ$.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Geometry and Trigonometry > SL 3.3—Angles of elevation and depression > Question Type 1: Finding angles using bearings
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 1: Finding angles using bearings
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The problem uses bearings (030° and 120°) to form a triangle between the ships and the port, requiring interpretation of bearings as angles.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Geometry and Trigonometry > SL 3.2—2d and 3d trig > Question Type 4: Using cosine rule to find the 3rd side
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 4: Using cosine rule to find the 3rd side
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: After converting bearings to the included angle between the two travel directions, the distance between ships is found using the cosine rule (third side of a triangle given two sides and included angle).

parts.content: Calculate the distance between the two ships after they complete their journeys.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate the angle between the ships: $120^\circ - 30^\circ = 90^\circ$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Identify the triangle properties: $a = 60$ km, $b = 80$ km, angle $= 90^\circ$ $\implies$ right-angled triangle <marks>A1</marks>
- Substitute into the Pythagorean theorem: $d^2 = 60^2 + 80^2$
- Calculate the distance: $d = 100$ km <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Calculate the included angle
steps.content:
To understand the relationship between the paths of the two ships, we first calculate the angle between their bearings. Since both ships start from the same port, the angle between them is simply the difference between their bearings:

$$\text{Angle} = 120^\circ - 030^\circ = 90^\circ$$

steps.title: Identify the triangle type
steps.content:
Because the angle between the two paths is exactly $90^\circ$, the journey forms a **right-angled triangle** where the distance from the port to ship A and the distance from the port to ship B are the two legs ($a$ and $b$). The distance between the two ships is the hypotenuse ($c$).

This means we can use the Pythagorean Theorem, which is a special case of the **Cosine Rule** found in your data booklet ($c^2 = a^2 + b^2 - 2ab \cos C$) when $C = 90^\circ$.

steps.title: Apply the Pythagorean Theorem
steps.content:
Now, substitute the known distances into the Pythagorean formula:

$$\begin{aligned} d^2 &= 60^2 + 80^2 \\ d^2 &= 3600 + 6400 \\ d^2 &= 10000 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate the final distance
steps.content:
To find the distance $d$, take the square root of both sides:

$$\begin{aligned} d &= \sqrt{10000} \\ d &= 100 \text{ km} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter √(60^2 + 80^2) into the main calculation screen and press execute.

calculator.description: Calculate the square root of the sum of squares to find the distance.

steps.title: Visualize the triangle
steps.content:
To find the distance between the two ships, we can model their journeys as two sides of a triangle starting from the same point (the port). 

Let:
- $a = 60$ km (distance of ship A)
- $b = 80$ km (distance of ship B)

We need to find the length of the third side, which represents the distance between them.

steps.title: Find the included angle
steps.content:
The angle between the two ships' paths is the difference between their bearings. Ship A is at $030^\circ$ and Ship B is at $120^\circ$.

$$\text{Angle } C = 120^\circ - 30^\circ = 90^\circ$$

steps.title: Apply the Cosine Rule
steps.content:
Since we have two sides and the included angle, we can use the **Cosine Rule** from your data booklet to find the distance $d$:

$$c^{2}=a^{2}+b^{2}-2ab\cos C$$

Substituting our values:

$$d^2 = 60^2 + 80^2 - 2(60)(80)\cos(90^\circ)$$

Note: Since $\cos(90^\circ) = 0$, the formula simplifies significantly (effectively becoming the Pythagorean theorem), but we will compute the full expression to be sure.

steps.title: Solve for distance
steps.content:
Now we calculate the numerical values:

$$\begin{aligned} d^2 &= 3600 + 6400 - 9600(0) \\ d^2 &= 10000 \\ d &= \sqrt{10000} \\ d &= 100 \text{ km} \end{aligned}$$

This calculation would earn you full marks: one for the angle, one for the correct substitution, and one for the final answer.

gdc.instructions: Use the square root and cosine functions. Check that the angle unit is set to degrees.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression into the home screen. Ensure your calculator is in DEGREE mode (Mode -> Degree).

desmos.instructions: Type the formula directly into a cell. Ensure 'Degrees' is selected in the settings menu.

calculator.description: Calculate the distance using the Cosine Rule expression.

steps.title: Set up the coordinate system
steps.content:
To solve this using coordinate geometry, let's place the port at the origin $(0, 0)$. In navigation, bearings are measured clockwise from North. We can represent the North-South direction as the $y$-axis and the East-West direction as the $x$-axis.

For a ship at distance $d$ on a bearing $	heta$, its coordinates $(x, y)$ are:
- $x = d \sin(\theta)$
- $y = d \cos(\theta)$ 

This maps North to the positive $y$-axis and East to the positive $x$-axis.

steps.title: Coordinates for Ship A
steps.content:
Ship A travels $60$ km on a bearing of $030^\circ$. Let's calculate its coordinates $(x_A, y_A)$:

$$\begin{aligned} x_A &= 60 \sin(30^\circ) = 30 \\ y_A &= 60 \cos(30^\circ) \approx 51.962 \end{aligned}$$

So, Ship A is at $(30, 51.962)$.

highlights.text: Ship A travels $60$ km on a bearing of $030^\circ$

steps.title: Coordinates for Ship B
steps.content:
Ship B travels $80$ km on a bearing of $120^\circ$. Let's calculate its coordinates $(x_B, y_B)$:

$$\begin{aligned} x_B &= 80 \sin(120^\circ) \approx 69.282 \\ y_B &= 80 \cos(120^\circ) = -40 \end{aligned}$$

So, Ship B is at $(69.282, -40)$.

highlights.text: ship B travels $80$ km on a bearing of $120^\circ$

steps.title: Apply the distance formula
steps.content:
Now we use the distance formula to find the separation between the two points $(30, 51.962)$ and $(69.282, -40)$. This concept is covered in **Topic 3: Geometry and Trigonometry (SL 3.1)**.

$$d = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2}$$

Substituting our coordinates:

$$\begin{aligned} d &= \sqrt{(69.282 - 30)^2 + (-40 - 51.962)^2} \\ d &= \sqrt{(39.282)^2 + (-91.962)^2} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate final distance
steps.content:
Now, let's compute the squares and the square root to find the distance.

$$\begin{aligned} d &= \sqrt{1543.07... + 8457.01...} \\ d &= \sqrt{10000.08...} \approx 100 \text{ km} \end{aligned}$$

The distance between the two ships is $100$ km.

gdc.instructions: Evaluate the square root of the squared differences of coordinates.

ti84.instructions: Enter the distance formula calculation directly into the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Input the distance formula using the coordinates found in steps 1 and 2.

calculator.description: Calculate the distance between Ship A and Ship B using the distance formula.

questions.specification: A ship travels 30 km due East, then turns and sails 40 km due North.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Geometry and Trigonometry > SL 3.3—Angles of elevation and depression > Question Type 1: Finding angles using bearings
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 1: Finding angles using bearings
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (c) asks for the direction as a bearing from the starting point, which is a direct application of bearings/angles in navigation contexts.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Geometry and Trigonometry > SL 3.2—2d and 3d trig > Question Type 2: Using SOH CAH TOA to find the angle given two sides
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 2: Using SOH CAH TOA to find the angle given two sides
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (b) requires the straight-line distance from the start after moving east then north, forming a right triangle and using basic trigonometry/Pythagoras (SOHCAHTOA context).

parts.content: Calculate the distance of the ship from its starting point.
parts.markscheme:
- Use the Pythagorean theorem to find the distance $d$: $d^2 = 30^2 + 40^2$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Calculate the final distance: $d = \sqrt{900 + 1600} = \sqrt{2500} = 50\text{ km}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Visualize the right-angled triangle
steps.content: The ship's journey consists of two perpendicular segments: moving East and then moving North. Since East and North are at a $90^{\circ}$ angle to each other, the path forms a right-angled triangle where the legs are the distances traveled and the hypotenuse is the direct distance from the starting point.

steps.title: Set up the Pythagorean Theorem
steps.content:
We can use the Pythagorean theorem to find the hypotenuse ($d$). The formula states that for a right triangle with legs $a$ and $b$ and hypotenuse $c$:

$$a^2 + b^2 = c^2$$

Substituting our values:

$$30^2 + 40^2 = d^2$$

steps.title: Solve for the distance
steps.content:
First, calculate the squares of the side lengths:

$$\begin{aligned} d^2 &= 900 + 1600 \\ d^2 &= 2500 \end{aligned}$$

To find the distance $d$, we take the square root of both sides:

$$d = \sqrt{2500} = 50\text{ km}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the square root function, then type 30^2 + 40^2 and execute.

calculator.description: Calculate the hypotenuse using the square root of the sum of squares.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The ship is 50 km away from its starting point.

In the markscheme, 1 mark (**M1**) is awarded for correctly setting up the Pythagorean equation ($30^2 + 40^2$), and 1 mark (**A1**) is awarded for the correct final answer of 50 km.

steps.title: Visualize the journey
steps.content: To find the distance from the starting point, we can model the ship's path as two sides of a triangle. The first leg is **30 km due East**, and the second leg is **40 km due North**. Since North and East are perpendicular, the angle between these two paths is $90^\circ$. We are looking for the third side of this triangle, which represents the direct distance from the start.

steps.title: State the Cosine Rule
steps.content:
While we often use the Pythagorean theorem for right-angled triangles, we can also use the more general **Cosine Rule** found in your data booklet. This rule works for any triangle and is written as:

$$c^2 = a^2 + b^2 - 2ab\cos C$$

In our case, $a = 30$, $b = 40$, and the included angle $C = 90^\circ$.

steps.title: Substitute and simplify
steps.content:
Now, let's substitute our values into the formula. A key thing to remember is that $\cos(90^\circ) = 0$. This makes the formula much simpler:

$$\begin{aligned} d^2 &= 30^2 + 40^2 - 2(30)(40)\cos(90^\circ) \\ d^2 &= 900 + 1600 - 2400(0) \\ d^2 &= 2500 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Input 30^2 + 40^2 - 2 * 30 * 40 * cos(90) into the run-matrix menu. Check that angle mode is set to Deg.

ti84.instructions: Enter 30² + 40² - 2 * 30 * 40 * cos(90). Ensure your calculator is in DEGREE mode.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into the cell and select 'Degrees' in the settings.

calculator.description: Calculate the value of the squared distance using the Cosine Rule.

steps.title: Calculate final distance
steps.content:
Finally, we take the square root of $2500$ to find the actual distance $d$:

$$d = \sqrt{2500} = 50\text{ km}$$

In an IB exam, you earn one mark for correctly setting up the equation and one mark for the final answer with units.

gdc.instructions: Use the square root function on 2500.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [x²] (the square root key) and type 2500.

calculator.description: Find the square root of the result.

steps.title: Visualize the right-angled triangle
steps.content:
First, let's look at the path of the ship. It travels East and then North, which are perpendicular directions. This creates a right-angled triangle where the two legs are the distances traveled, and the distance from the starting point is the hypotenuse.

- Horizontal leg (East): $30\text{ km}$
- Vertical leg (North): $40\text{ km}$

steps.title: Identify the scale factor
steps.content:
Instead of squaring large numbers, we can look for a common factor between the two sides. 

Both $30$ and $40$ are multiples of $10$:
$$\begin{aligned} 30 &= 3 \times 10 \\ 40 &= 4 \times 10 \end{aligned}$$

Notice that the sides are in a $3:4$ ratio. This is a huge clue that we are working with a **3-4-5 Pythagorean triple**.

steps.title: Apply the 3-4-5 ratio
steps.content:
A famous Pythagorean triple is $3, 4, 5$, where $3^2 + 4^2 = 5^2$. 

Since our triangle's legs are $3 \times 10$ and $4 \times 10$, the hypotenuse must follow the same scale factor ($k = 10$):

$$\text{Hypotenuse} = 5 \times 10 = 50\text{ km}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter sqrt(30^2 + 40^2) in the calculation menu.

desmos.instructions: Type sqrt(30^2 + 40^2) into the expression bar.

calculator.description: Verify the calculation using the Pythagorean theorem directly.

steps.title: State the final distance
steps.content:
The distance of the ship from its starting point is $50\text{ km}$.

In an IB exam, showing the identification of the $3:4:5$ ratio or writing down the Pythagorean theorem formula earns you the method mark (**M1**), and the correct final value earns the accuracy mark (**A1**).

parts.content: Calculate the direction of the ship from its starting point as a bearing.
parts.markscheme:
- Use the tangent ratio to find the angle $\theta$ from the North-South line: $\tan(\theta) = \frac{30}{40}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- State the bearing as a three-digit figure to 3 significant figures: $036.9^\circ$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<examTip>Remember to express the bearing as a three-digit figure, measured clockwise from North.</examTip>

steps.title: Visualize the direction
steps.content:
To find the bearing from the starting point, we visualize a right-angled triangle. A bearing is measured clockwise from the North-South line. 

In this scenario, the ship's path creates a triangle where:
*   The **Northward** displacement ($40\text{ km}$) is the **adjacent** side to the angle $\theta$ at the start.
*   The **Eastward** displacement ($30\text{ km}$) is the **opposite** side to the angle $\theta$.

steps.title: Set up tangent ratio
steps.content:
Using the Trigonometry from North-South Line approach, we use the tangent ratio. This relates the opposite side (East) to the adjacent side (North) to find the angle $\theta$ measured clockwise from North:

$$\tan(\theta) = \frac{\text{Opposite}}{\text{Adjacent}}$$

Substituting our known values:

$$\tan(\theta) = \frac{30}{40}$$

steps.title: Solve for the angle
steps.content:
To find the angle $\theta$, we use the inverse tangent function $(\tan^{-1})$ on your calculator:

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{30}{40}\right)$$

$$\theta \approx 36.869...^\circ$$

gdc.instructions: Use the inverse tangent function in degree mode.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [TAN], then type 30 / 40 and press [ENTER]. Ensure the calculator is in DEGREE mode (check [MODE]).

desmos.instructions: Type arctan(30/40) and ensure the setting is set to 'Degrees' in the Graph Settings (wrench icon).

calculator.description: Calculate the inverse tangent to find the angle in degrees.

steps.title: State the final bearing
steps.content:
A bearing must be expressed as a three-digit figure. Since the angle is $36.9^\circ$ (to 3 significant figures), we add a leading zero.

$$\text{Bearing} = 036.9^\circ$$

highlights.text: State the bearing as a three-digit figure to 3 significant figures: 036.9^\circ
Remember to express the bearing as a three-digit figure, measured clockwise from North.

steps.title: Visualize the right-angled triangle
steps.content:
To find the direction, we can imagine the ship's journey as a right-angled triangle. 

*   The ship first travels **30 km due East** (the horizontal component).
*   It then turns and travels **40 km due North** (the vertical component).

We want to find the direction from the starting point to the final position. This is covered in **Topic 3: Geometry and Trigonometry**.

steps.title: Find angle North of East
steps.content:
In our approach, we first calculate the angle $\alpha$ that the ship's path makes with the **East-West line** (the horizontal). 

In our triangle:
*   The **Opposite** side is the Northward distance: $40\text{ km}$
*   The **Adjacent** side is the Eastward distance: $30\text{ km}$

Using the tangent ratio from SOH CAH TOA:

$$\tan(\alpha) = \frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{adjacent}} = \frac{40}{30}$$

steps.title: Calculate the angle alpha
steps.content:
Now, we use the inverse tangent function to find the value of $\alpha$:

$$\alpha = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{40}{30}\right) \approx 53.13...^\circ$$

This is the angle measured **North of East**.

gdc.instructions: Set angle mode to degrees. Enter atan(40/30).

ti84.instructions: Ensure your calculator is in DEGREE mode. Press [2nd] [TAN] (40 / 30) [ENTER].

calculator.description: Calculate the inverse tangent of 40/30 to find the angle from the East-West line.

steps.title: Convert to a bearing
steps.content:
A **bearing** is measured clockwise from North. 

Since North is $000^\circ$ and East is $090^\circ$, we can find the bearing by subtracting our angle $\alpha$ from $90^\circ$:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Bearing} &= 90^\circ - \alpha \\ \text{Bearing} &= 90^\circ - 53.13...^\circ = 36.87...^\circ \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Subtract the calculated angle from 90 to find the bearing from North.

steps.title: State final bearing
steps.content:
Finally, we round our answer to **3 significant figures** and format it as a **three-digit figure** as required for bearings.

$$36.87...^\circ \approx 036.9^\circ$$

highlights.text: Remember to express the bearing as a three-digit figure, measured clockwise from North.
